cold_watch.immediate_felt_reaction: The opening feels like a fast kitchen-table diagnosis: the creator turns not eating greens into concrete daily symptoms with labeled cups and a direct headline (ev_keyframe_001, ev_ocr_001).
cold_watch.moment_of_curiosity: Curiosity peaks when each symptom cup gets mapped to a nutrient in red overlays, starting with the 'Can't poop?' to zinc pairing and continuing through energy, brain fog, and body aches (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_011).
cold_watch.moment_of_confusion_or_boredom: The weakest early moment is the long symptom-to-nutrient sequence, because several health associations are asserted quickly without substantiation beyond the creator's props and overlays (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_009, ev_keyframe_011).
cold_watch.most_memorable_image: The most memorable image is the row of plastic cups with sticky-note symptoms, followed by the Gruns packet being positioned as the item that contains the listed vitamins and minerals (ev_keyframe_001, ev_keyframe_012, ev_keyframe_014).

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator stands in a kitchen behind five clear cups labeled with symptom notes; the top caption says 'If you don't eat your GREENS..' and the first cup reads 'CAN'T POOP?'. A red overlay maps 'Can't [poop]?' to 'Zinc'.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The viewer is pushed into a quick self-check: the ad reframes a vague nutrition habit as a list of embarrassing or uncomfortable daily problems.

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator repeats the cup reveal pattern: 'Feeling sick?' maps to vitamin A and C, 'Low energy?' maps to iron, 'Brain fog?' maps to vitamin E, and 'Body aches?' maps to niacin/niacine in red overlays.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The repetition turns scattered symptoms into a checklist, making the viewer wait for their own problem to appear.

evidence_timeline.observation: The camera switches to handheld product footage near the cups and a Gruns packet; captions say 'if you guys didn't know' and 'these Gruns gummies have all those vitamins and minerals I just listed' while the bag's supplement facts panel is shown.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The ad tries to convert the symptom checklist into a bundled-product answer, using the product label as a proof cue.

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator holds a Gruns daily packet close to camera while captions say it 'actually tastes really good,' is 'easy to eat,' and that 'you and your kids will enjoy them'.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: After the health-problem setup, the ad reduces usage friction by promising taste, ease, and family acceptance.

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator opens a large Gruns bag full of small packets; captions say 'if you wanna grab yourself this huge bag,' 'that comes with 28 of these small packs,' and 'I put a link right here for you'.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The product moves from health answer to tangible purchase unit: a big bag, many daily packs, and a direct link cue.

evidence_timeline.observation: The creator returns to a close-up with several packets and captions say there is a 'massive sale,' it 'ends in a couple days,' the product has been 'selling out like crazy,' and viewers should 'try to get them if you can'.
evidence_timeline.viewer_perception: The ad closes with scarcity and urgency, but the sale and sell-out claims are not independently proven in the video.

core_mechanism.name: symptom checklist to bundled supplement answer
core_mechanism.why_it_catches_attention: It opens with a direct, slightly provocative greens headline and immediately turns nutrition avoidance into visible symptom props on a kitchen counter.
core_mechanism.why_it_might_persuade: It gives viewers a simple ladder: recognize a symptom, see a nutrient association, then see a single product framed as containing the listed vitamins and minerals.
core_mechanism.what_cannot_be_copied: Do not copy the Gruns brand, health claims, symptom-to-nutrient mappings, creator likeness, kitchen setup, captions, music, sale language, or scarcity claims.

creative_intent.creative_type: UGC / creator testimonial / direct-response supplement offer
creative_intent.likely_hypothesis: If viewers connect skipped greens with common discomforts, one daily packet will feel like a convenient fix and increase click or purchase intent.
creative_intent.target_audience: Health-curious, convenience-seeking adults or parents who know they should eat greens but do not consistently do it.
creative_intent.funnel_stage: Cold prospecting direct-response creative with conversion intent.
creative_intent.likely_kpi: Click-through, offer-page visits, first purchase, CAC, and ROAS.
creative_intent.offer_strategy: Use a symptom self-check to create personal relevance, then use product proof and sale urgency to push action.
creative_intent.marketer_job_to_be_done: Turn a vague nutrition habit into an urgent, personal reason to try a convenient supplement pack.
unknowns: No media-buy targeting, landing-page analytics, spend, CAC, ROAS, or conversion data were provided.
The report infers funnel and KPI from visible creative signals only.

distilled_core.core_thesis: The creative converts a vague greens habit into a symptom checklist, then positions one packet as the convenient bundled answer.
distilled_core.mechanism_formula: greens neglect -> symptom self-check -> nutrient mapping -> packet proof -> taste/ease reassurance -> scarcity CTA
distilled_core.belief_shift_summary: The viewer is moved from 'greens are optional' to 'my daily discomforts might be solvable through one easy supplement routine.'
distilled_core.why_it_works: It uses self-diagnosis and visible props to make an abstract nutrition promise feel immediate, personal, and easy to act on.
distilled_core.where_it_breaks: The proof is thin because symptom-to-nutrient links, family acceptance, sale urgency, and sell-out claims are asserted rather than verified.
distilled_core.submagic_transfer: Translate the structure into creator-workflow pain: labeled editing bottlenecks become a visible Submagic feature ladder and proof scene.
steal: Open with a concrete self-check instead of a generic product promise.
Use repeated physical labels to make invisible pain feel inspectable.
Bridge from pain to product with a clear bundled-solution moment.
avoid: Do not borrow the brand, health claims, creator identity, captions, or scarcity language.
Do not make unsupported performance or outcome claims.
Do not let the diagnostic ladder run longer than the proof can support.
test_next: Test a shorter three-problem ladder against the full five-problem ladder.
Test putting the product proof before the fourth symptom card.
Test verified social proof instead of unverified sell-out urgency.

core_understanding.human_truth: People often know they should eat better, but they respond faster when a broad health habit is connected to a specific annoyance they already feel.

belief_shift.before: Not eating greens is a vague wellness miss that can be postponed.
belief_shift.after: Not eating greens may be tied to concrete discomforts, and one easy daily packet may cover the nutrients the viewer is missing.
belief_shift.bridge: The ad bridges the gap with sticky-note symptom props, fast nutrient overlays, visible product packaging, and a scarcity CTA.

tension_stack: Fear: the viewer may recognize one of the symptom labels and feel they are neglecting a basic health habit (ev_keyframe_001, ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_011).
Desire: the viewer wants a simpler routine than cooking or consistently eating greens (ev_keyframe_016, ev_ocr_016).
Objection: supplements can taste bad or be hard for a family to adopt, so the creator claims taste, ease, and kid acceptance (ev_keyframe_015, ev_keyframe_017).
Proof need: the product must plausibly contain the listed nutrients, so the bag and supplement facts panel are shown, though the label is not fully legible (ev_keyframe_013, ev_keyframe_014).
Urgency pressure: the ad adds sale, deadline, and sell-out claims at the end, but those claims are unverified inside the video (ev_keyframe_021, ev_keyframe_022, ev_keyframe_023, ev_keyframe_024).

proof_ladder.claim: Skipping greens is associated in the creative with common discomforts.
proof_ladder.weakness: The association is a persuasive setup, not verified medical proof.

proof_ladder.claim: Each named discomfort can be mapped to a nutrient.
proof_ladder.proof_type: red overlay mappings beside labeled cups
proof_ladder.weakness: The mappings are asserted quickly without citations or dosage context.

proof_ladder.claim: The product contains the vitamins and minerals just listed.
proof_ladder.proof_type: product packet, bag, and supplement facts panel
proof_ladder.weakness: The panel is visible but not readable enough to independently verify every listed nutrient.

proof_ladder.claim: The product reduces adoption friction by tasting good and being easy to eat.
proof_ladder.proof_type: creator testimonial captions with packet held close to camera
proof_ladder.weakness: Taste and ease are subjective claims from the creator.

proof_ladder.claim: The purchase unit offers many small daily packs.
proof_ladder.weakness: The video shows multiple packs but does not independently prove the exact count.

proof_ladder.claim: There is urgency because of a sale, deadline, and sell-out pressure.
proof_ladder.weakness: No inventory, price, or campaign source is shown.

viewer_state_timeline.trigger: A blunt greens headline and symptom cups make the viewer scan for a problem they recognize.
The creator repeats symptom-to-nutrient reveals across multiple cups.
The product packet and supplement facts appear after the symptom list.
Taste, ease, and family enjoyment are stated while the packet is held in hand.
The bag is opened and the 28 small packs claim is introduced before the link cue.
The closing captions stack sale, deadline, sell-out, and 'get them if you can' language.

core_understanding.skeptic_read: A skeptical viewer may reject the ad because the nutrient mappings, health implications, sale deadline, and sell-out pressure are visible claims but not substantiated with citations, pricing, inventory proof, or third-party validation.
core_understanding.transfer_principle_for_submagic: Use labeled pains as a diagnostic ladder, then collapse them into one visible workflow proof scene that only Submagic can own: captions, cuts, zooms, B-roll, brand styling, and publish-ready output.
counterfactuals: If the product proof appeared earlier, the ad would likely feel less like unsupported symptom mapping and more like a concrete supplement demo.
If the symptom ladder used fewer examples, the hook could feel sharper and leave more time for credible proof.
If the urgency close included visible price or inventory context, the scarcity claim would feel less generic.
If the creator opened with the bag instead of the symptom cups, the ad would likely lose its strongest self-diagnostic hook.

scores.improvement_note: The hook is visually legible and problem-specific, but the first proof should arrive sooner.
The cups make the pain stack easy to scan.
The supplement facts panel is a proof cue, but the claims are not readable or independently supported.
The packet and bag are repeatedly shown after the setup.
Taste and ease are addressed, but they remain creator assertions.
The repeated symptom pattern builds momentum but may overstay before the product appears.
The CTA is explicit, but the sale and sell-out proof is unsupported.
The surface execution is heavily tied to the competitor brand, product claims, and creator setup; only the mechanism should transfer.

tiktok.notes: Native talking-head framing, large captions, prop-led hook, and direct link language fit TikTok patterns, though health proof remains weak (ev_keyframe_001, ev_keyframe_020, ev_keyframe_024).

reels.notes: The vertical kitchen demo and readable top captions fit Reels, but the long symptom ladder may need tighter compression for cold paid placements (ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_011).

shorts.notes: The self-check hook can work on Shorts, but product proof does not appear clearly until around 9.415s, which may be late for low-intent viewers (ev_keyframe_012).

meta_paid.notes: Meta paid would likely need stronger compliance review and substantiation for symptom, nutrient, sale, and sell-out claims (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_014, ev_keyframe_021, ev_keyframe_023).

linkedin.notes: The consumer supplement hook, bathroom-adjacent symptom language, and scarcity close do not naturally fit LinkedIn's business context (ev_keyframe_001, ev_keyframe_024).

drop_off_hypotheses: Viewers who do not identify with the first symptom may wait for the next cup, but the repeated ladder could lose them before product proof appears at 9.415s (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_012).
Health-skeptical viewers may drop when nutrient mappings are asserted without sources or dosage context (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_011).
Viewers who dislike scarcity tactics may discount the final sale and sell-out claims because no external proof is shown (ev_keyframe_021, ev_keyframe_022, ev_keyframe_023, ev_keyframe_024).
risks_and_unknowns: No performance metrics were provided, so this report treats all judgments as creative-signal hypotheses only.

The video contains health-adjacent symptom and nutrient claims; this deconstruction records the claims as visible ad content, not as medical facts (ev_keyframe_002, ev_keyframe_005, ev_keyframe_008, ev_keyframe_011).
The sale, deadline, and sell-out claims are visible in captions but not independently verified (ev_keyframe_021, ev_keyframe_022, ev_keyframe_023, ev_keyframe_024).
OCR was noisy for several mid-roll and end-card captions, so keyframe inspection carries more weight than raw OCR in those sections (ev_ocr_012, ev_ocr_018, ev_ocr_022, ev_ocr_024).

Only the mechanism should be adapted for Submagic; the brand identity, product claims, exact symptom labels, creator likeness, kitchen setup, caption wording, and urgency language should not be copied.
